coredata.dc:title: Prospects of sustainable and renewable source-based carbon quantum dots for food packaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Ezati, Parya
Priyadarshi, Ruchir
Rhim, Jong-Whan
coredata.dc:description: CQDs are relatively new nanomaterials discovered accidentally during the purification process of carbon nanotubes. Since then, several attempts have been made to synthesize CQDs from various sources, particularly economically and sustainably. Carbon is one of the most important life support elements and is widely distributed as a major component in all living and non-living organisms. The recent discovery that CQD is produced during the normal cooking process has provided an innovative dimension in the sustainable production of CQD from waste food or food by-products. CQDs produced during the cooking process or from food processing wastes are similar to those produced chemically and have been successfully used for sensing and bio-imaging due to their characteristic photoluminescence. However, other functional properties of CQDs, such as antibacterial and antioxidant activity, have been reported recently and have not yet been widely utilized. Moreover, the low toxicity of CQDs has not been sufficiently discussed. This review focuses on the prospect of CQDs as functional materials for active food packaging applications by manufacturing CQDs with various functionalities from food waste or food processing by-products. The concept of incorporating CQDs into active films could be an emerging active food packaging revolution. Therefore, it is worthwhile to study new information on the proficiency of CQD-based films in emerging inexpensive and sustainable active packaging materials. The present review briefly describes the synthetic methods, various functionalizations, and current CQD applications. Additionally, their potential use as sustainable, low-cost, value-added multifunctional fillers in food packaging films and their mechanisms were discussed along with suggested and supported research data. The prospects of CQD will guide researchers to develop new materials for active food packaging applications.

head.ce:title: Prospects of sustainable and renewable source-based carbon quantum dots for food packaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CQDs are relatively new nanomaterials discovered accidentally during the purification process of carbon nanotubes. Since then, several attempts have been made to synthesize CQDs from various sources, particularly economically and sustainably. Carbon is one of the most important life support elements and is widely distributed as a major component in all living and non-living organisms. The recent discovery that CQD is produced during the normal cooking process has provided an innovative dimension in the sustainable production of CQD from waste food or food by-products. CQDs produced during the cooking process or from food processing wastes are similar to those produced chemically and have been successfully used for sensing and bio-imaging due to their characteristic photoluminescence. However, other functional properties of CQDs, such as antibacterial and antioxidant activity, have been reported recently and have not yet been widely utilized. Moreover, the low toxicity of CQDs has not been sufficiently discussed. This review focuses on the prospect of CQDs as functional materials for active food packaging applications by manufacturing CQDs with various functionalities from food waste or food processing by-products. The concept of incorporating CQDs into active films could be an emerging active food packaging revolution. Therefore, it is worthwhile to study new information on the proficiency of CQD-based films in emerging inexpensive and sustainable active packaging materials. The present review briefly describes the synthetic methods, various functionalizations, and current CQD applications. Additionally, their potential use as sustainable, low-cost, value-added multifunctional fillers in food packaging films and their mechanisms were discussed along with suggested and supported research data. The prospects of CQD will guide researchers to develop new materials for active food packaging applications.

Medium particle-sized chlorhexidine gluconate carbon dots
Large particle-sized chlorhexidine gluconate carbon dots
Nitrogen and phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: Ensuring food safety and security is one of the biggest challenges faced by the global community. Every country spends a significant portion of its economy producing food for its people. However, a significant portion of food is wasted due to spoilage before it reaches consumers, resulting in huge economic losses [1]. Food spoilage during storage and distribution is mainly due to the growth of microorganisms or biochemical actions such as the oxidation of food ingredients. As one of the food packaging technologies to secure food safety and extend shelf life, active packaging attempts to prevent food spoilage and reduce food loss after processing, gaining popularity recently [2]. Active packaging includes the deliberate addition of active substances such as antibacterial agents and antioxidants to packaging materials. These active ingredients interact with the packaged food or product headspace to complement the basic function of the packaging material [3,4]. Therefore, active functional materials with excellent functionality and safety are essential for the successful development of active packaging materials.

Recently, QDs have emerged as a new kind of functional material. Zero-dimensional nanoparticles, typically <10 nm, exhibit characteristic optoelectronic properties by providing three-dimensional limited electron motion [5–8].

During the past decade, the photoluminescent properties of various non-metal-based quantum dots have also been exploited in human-contact biomedical applications such as cellular imaging [9,10]. The advent of these environmentally friendly and low toxicity quantum dots opens up new possibilities for biological and diverse applications [11]. In addition, enormous research efforts have been put into applying QD in food packaging technology. This field of quantum dots is advancing into a new era after the advent of nanomaterials, especially CQDs. Recently, we reported CQDs' antibacterial and antioxidant activity, opening up new areas for using these quantum materials as active fillers in food packaging [12,13].

In this regard, CQD has emerged as a viable option due to its easy and sustainable synthetic process from various bio-based sources such as food waste, agricultural waste, polysaccharides, microorganisms, and benign carbonaceous chemicals [14]. CQD applications are rapidly improving in terms of synthesis and applications due to their excellent properties of chemical and photostability, tunable high-fluorescence, non-toxic/low toxicity, and biocompatibility. Active additives commonly used to fabricate nanocomposite films are manufactured by chemical routes, making them expensive and relatively unsafe for direct or indirect human contact applications [15,16]. However, the main advantage of CQD is that it can be easily prepared from a large number of biomass/waste suitable for the developing trend of cheap, environmentally friendly, sustainable, green, safe, and functional additives.

However, the potential applications of CQDs as active and intelligent packaging materials have been previously reviewed. Nevertheless, its safety and packaging applications in food items are not fully discussed. This review presents the perspective of applying these value-added CQDs to food packaging as a sustainable and inexpensive multifunctional additive to impart functional properties. The current applications focused on the properties and utilization of CQDs are discussed.

Various methods are used to synthesize CQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . Strategies for CQD synthesis methods tend to seek sustainable and environmentally friendly manufacturing procedures that can influence the surface function and size of CQDs, which play an essential role in determining the properties of CQDs and their interactions with biological surfaces. CQD synthesis methods are classified into top-down and bottom-up methods [14,17]. For top-down processes, large carbon materials such as graphite, graphene, and activated carbon are cut into CQDs through a process that includes multiple steps, including exfoliation and fragmentation [18]. However, most of these methods have limitations in precisely controlling the shape and size of nanoparticles [19,20]. Small molecules are converted to CQDs using bottom-up methods through chemical reactions such as carbonization, degradation, and fusion procedures. Unlike the top-down method, all processes of CQD synthesis can be performed within one step, making it suitable for large-scale production. It meets the green chemistry principle regarding simplicity, efficiency, cost, and energy consumption [21]. These features prompted early researchers to pursue bottom-up methods for CQD fabrication. In particular, the hydrothermal carbonization method is being utilized with the advantages of self-generated pressure and subcritical temperature (180–250 °C), which is considered a promising method from the point of view of energy saving. Importantly, water is the most widely used catalyst and solvent in this method. Its compatibility and low cost may make it an excellent choice for developing functional packaging films [22].

Carbon is one of the important elements for sustaining life. All living things on earth are composed of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats, whose main element is carbon. Therefore, all biological species are carbon sources in some form, and organic food is also a rich carbon source. This carbon can be utilized in the form of CQD from food waste that is mass-produced worldwide. According to the Food Waste Index Report 2021, 931 million tons of food are wasted worldwide [23]. This food waste can be used as a raw material for CQD synthesis to produce sustainable and high-value-added functional materials. There have been many studies on detecting and isolating CQDs in various food sources such as meat, poultry, seafood, dairy, etc. This section focuses on different methods to isolate CQDs from different food sources.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs produced in the cooking process
ce:section.ce:para: The first discovery of CQDs was accidentally discovered in 2004 by Xu et al. while trying to purify carbon nanotube fragments [24]. Later, Sk et al. found the presence of CQDs in caramel and reported that these quantum substances were created during the daily cooking process of food [25]. In 2014, Jiang et al. found strong fluorescence in instant coffee due to CQDs [26]. Liao et al. also reported the presence of fluorescent CQDs in several commercial beverages in 2015 [27]. Several cooked and processed foods have been used for CQD synthesis, including roast duck, lamb, eel, salmon, swordfish, bread, grilled hamburgers, pizza, barbecue, beer, cola, kvass, coffee, caramel, and honey [28].

Commonly used cooking processes, such as baking, roasting, or grilling, create conditions similar to pyrolytic decomposition, converting the carbon present in food into CQDs. Ye et al. reported that nitrogen and sulfur co-doped fluorescent CQDs were formed through pyrolytic carbonization of eggs [29]. Consequently, it is known that hydrothermal treatment of foods with hot aqueous solutions at high vapor pressures can produce CQDs. In addition, they are produced by the Maillard reaction, a non-enzymatic browning reaction that normally occurs during cooking. Since almost all foods are composed of protein and sugar, a Maillard reaction occurs between the nucleophilic amino group of the protein and the carbonyl group of the reducing sugar during food processing. Wei et al. demonstrated the formation of CQDs due to the Maillard reaction between various amino acids and glucose. Other reports of CQDs occurring during food processing show that microwave treatment efficiently synthesizes CQDs [30]. This method results in transient heating of the food, and the resulting CQD is presumed to have improved properties. Several research groups have reported using this method to synthesize CQDs. Wang, Zhu, et al. (2016) reported the synthesis of CQDs using milk as a carbon source [31], whereas Monte-Filho et al. (2019) synthesized CQDs using onions and lemons [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Production of CQDs from various food sources
ce:section.ce:para: The structure of CQDs consists of a carbonaceous core with functional groups on the surface responsible for CQDs' functional and biological properties. As already discussed, food consists mainly of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats. Carbohydrates and fats are the carbon sources for CQDs, and proteins are most important for surface functionalization due to different elements and amino acids with functional groups. Thus, it can be said that different food items serve as sources of CQDs with different functional properties, depending on their protein content and the types of amino acids present in the protein. For example, eggs are a rich source of proteins such as ovalbumin, ovotransferrin, lipovitellins, livetins, etc., hence can be used as a source for synthesizing N-functionalized CQD. Various methods have been applied to synthesize fluorescent CQDs using eggs. Ye et al. (2017) synthesized CQDs using a pyrolysis method using egg whites and yolks from pigeons and geese [29]. Hu et al. (2016) fabricated CQDs with silver sizes <3.2 nm from albumin using a microwave irradiation method [33]. Jusuf et al. (2018) produced CQDs from egg yolk using the hydrothermal synthesis method [34], whereas Zhang et al. (2015) prepared N-doped CQDs using the same method except for egg white (albumin) as a C-source [35]. These results further confirm the effect of raw material composition on CQD functionalization. Because albumin is high in protein, the resulting CQDs were N-functionalized instead of those prepared using yolks with higher carbon content in the lipid form. In addition to egg yolk and albumin, the eggshell membrane is also rich in sulfur-rich amino acids such as cysteine. Because eggshells are produced in large quantities as waste from the poultry and egg processing industries, they can be used as an economical source for synthesizing N- or S-functionalized CQDs. Pramanik et al. (2018) synthesized CQDs from chicken eggshells using combined methods of pyrolysis and sonication, and the resulting CQDs were N/S functionalized and had a size of 3.3 nm [36].

Milk and dairy products were also used as sources of CQD synthesis. For this purpose, the hydrothermal method [37,38] and the microwave-assisted method [37,39] were mainly used. The milk and dairy industry produces large amounts of waste, including whey, dairy sludge, and wastewater, that can be used for the sustainable synthesis of CQD. These wastes or byproducts, especially whey protein, are rich in sulfur-containing amino acids such as cysteine and methionine. Das et al. (2016) demonstrated the facile synthesis of N/S functionalized CQDs from whey protein using a single-step microwave-assisted treatment [40].

Poultry products are also a rich source of protein and can be used to synthesize N and S-functionalized CQDs. Cong et al. (2019) synthesized CQDs by roasting duck breast at 200–300 °C [41]. They found that the size of synthesized CQDs decreased with increasing roasting temperature.

Another industry that produces excess by-product waste is seafood, where inedible parts such as shells, scales, and fins are discarded during seafood processing. Being a rich protein source, it has proven to be an excellent precursor material for the sustainable production of N-functionalized CQDs [14]. Gedda et al. (2016) used hydrothermal treatment of shrimp shell powder at 200 °C for 8 h and produced fluorescent CQDs with a size of ∼4 nm [42]. Using a similar method, Zhang, Gao, et al. (2018) synthesized N-functionalized CQD from a collagen-rich grass carp scale by treating it at 200 °C for 20 h [43]. In addition, the synthesis of CQDs with multiple surface functional groups using crab shell powder has been reported [44,45]. The main components of crabs, lobsters, and shrimp shells are chitin and chitosan, polysaccharides rich in N [46,47]. Zhang et al. (2021) used chitin for CQD synthesis and showed that N-atoms are incorporated into carbocyclic rings to increase CQDs' function and quantum yield [49]. An innovative approach was performed by Su et al. (2019), who added KOH/urea to the chitin solution to enhance its degradation during the CQD synthesis [50].

The studies conducted so far provide the following conclusions:
•
The size of the CQD is determined by the synthesis method and reaction conditions. For example, as the carbonization temperature increases, the CQD size decreases.
•
The protein content of food/food waste is primarily responsible for the surface functional groups of CQDs that determine their function and biological activity.
•
The amino acid type of a protein affects CQD function. Proteins rich in S-rich amino acids, such as cysteine and methionine, provide S-functionalizations, while other proteins provide N-functionalizations.
Food waste and by-products from the food processing industry can serve as sustainable, economical, and high-value-added raw materials for the green synthesis of highly fluorescent CQDs.

The chemical structure of CQDs usually depends on the source, which can influence the formation process, reaction parameters, and properties. Therefore, there are various methods to characterize the structure of CQDs. Fig. 2 shows the characterization method and properties of CQDs. Specifically, TEM, EDX, and elemental mapping provide morphology, aspect ratios, size distributions, and compositions of the CQDs. In most cases, TEM images showed a quasi-spherical shape for CQDs in the 1–10 nm range [51,52]. AFM is used to determine CQDs' surface roughness or thickness. The crystal planes of CQDs are further measured by XRD and SAED. In an XRD pattern of CQDs, a peak for highly amorphous carbon is detected at 23°, while two other peaks located at 25° and 44° are associated with (002) and (100) diffraction revealing a low-graphitic carbon structure [52]. The general structure, surface functional groups, and the interaction of the dopant with the CQDs are analyzed by FTIR spectroscopy [18]. XPS is usually used for surface analysis and compositional studies to determine the elemental composition of CQDs and the chemical nature of heteroatoms. The high-resolution spectra of C 1 s presented peaks at 284.8, 286.5, and 288.2 eV, corresponding to carbon atoms [53]. The thermal stability of CQDs is evaluated using TGA. UV–visible light absorption spectra are used to evaluate the light absorption of CQDs. The light absorption peak at 230 nm specifies the π to π* transition, while bands at 310 and 355 nm indicate the n-π* transition on the CQDs surface. Profluorescence emission spectra provide the PL property of CQDs. PL emission peak was observed at 450 nm after excitation at 355 nm [54].

Quantum dots have gained popularity due to their unique optical properties not found in other nanomaterials. The term “quantum” refers to a discrete unit of physical property. CQDs provide a three-dimensional restricted electronic motion such that quantum mechanics dictate these nanoparticles' properties [13,55]. This quantum confinement effect is responsible for the excitation-dependent photoluminescence of CQDs. The absorption characteristics of CQDs are source-dependent, but the characteristic light absorption is usually in the UV region (230–320 nm). For the carbon core, the absorption bands are ascribed to the π-π* transition of aromatic CC bonds or the n-π* transition of C=O/C=N bonds [56]. It is known that the absorption properties of CQDs are affected by changes in the type and content of surface groups, the size of the π-conjugated domains, and the oxygen/nitrogen content of the carbon core [57]. CQDs emit blue or green fluorescence in response to absorption, and PL emission generally shifts red with increasing excitation wavelength. Therefore, the PL of CDs can be easily tuned by controlling the excitation wavelength without changing the chemical structure or size, which is beneficial for multicolor bioimaging. Nevertheless, the application of CQDs is mostly limited to biomedical imaging due to the confinement of PL emission in the blue-green region. However, there are reports of synthesizing CQDs emitting at higher wavelengths, such as the NIR region, by adjusting the reaction conditions and carbon sources (e.g., aromatic compounds) [57].

CQD's excellent antimicrobial activity against a wide spectrum of microbial strains has generated great interest in food safety and biomedical applications in the past decade. Several studies have acknowledged the efficacy of CQDs in biomedical applications, including drug delivery, bioimaging, bio-labeling, and photodynamic therapy, and their safety concerning future human contact applications. In addition to biocompatibility and water solubility, CQDs show beneficial properties such as controllable surface functional groups and adsorption interactions due to their small size attached to biological surfaces for antibacterial purposes. Several recent studies have reported the potent antimicrobial activity of CQDs against a wide range of microorganisms, bacteria, fungi, and viruses. The antimicrobial activity of CQDs is attributed to the presence of surface functional groups on CQDs and their surface charge, size, and shape [58]. In this regard, Bing et al. (2016) compared the effect of the surface charge of CQDs, including negative, positive, and neutral charges, on the antibacterial action against E. coli [59]. They found that anionic and neutral CQDs had a weaker effect on bacteria, while cationic CQDs showed the highest activity against E. coli. Therefore, it can be said that the electrical properties of CQDs play a decisive role in antibacterial action. Cationic CQDs attract negative charges in bacterial cells, and anionic and neutral CQDs are repelled by bacterial cells. Kousheh et al. (2020) synthesized CQDs and evaluated their antibacterial action against pathogenic bacteria [60]. They reported higher antibacterial action against L. monocytogenes than E. coli due to the electrostatic repulsive forces between the bacteria and CQDs.

On the other hand, Lin et al. (2019) demonstrated antimicrobial activity through adhesion between bacterial cells and CQDs and overcame electrostatic repulsion by the attractive van der Waals forces [61]. Moreover, the studies so far also suggest that differences in cell wall structure that interact differently with doping materials and dopants can significantly affect the antimicrobial activity of CQDs against different microbial strains. For instance, Travlou et al. (2018) synthesized S-CQD and N-CQD and evaluated their antibacterial activity against Gram-positive (Bacillus subtilis subsp. subtilis 168) and Gram-negative (Escherichia coli, CECT 831) bacteria using MIC quantitative and qualitative disk diffusion test [62]. They reported excellent antimicrobial activity of S-CQD and N-CQD against both test bacteria with a MIC of 32 mg/mL. Electrostatic interaction between the protonated form of doped CQDs and target molecules in the bacterial cell wall causes bacterial death. In another study, Ezati et al. (2022) synthesized CQDs using glucose and heteroatom (boron, sulfur, and nitrogen atoms) doped CQDs and compared their antibacterial and antifungal activity against a wide range of microorganisms [63]. They found that the heteroatom-doped CQDs exhibited stronger antimicrobial activity due to specific functional groups, including amides and sulfonic functional groups. Particularly, N-doped CQD showed several times higher antimicrobial activity against bacteria (L. monocytogenes, E. coli), mold (A. fumigatus, F. solani, P. citrinum), and yeast (C. albicans, R. rubra) strains (Fig. 3a). It has also been suggested that doped CQDs have higher interactions with microbial cells due to additional ROS generation and thus have higher antimicrobial activity. SEM images of CQD-treated bacterial and fungal cells revealed significantly distorted cellular structures with low bacterial populations (Fig. 3b). Overall, the combined action of different mechanisms concerning the antimicrobial action of CQDs was demonstrated as followings:

(i) Production of ROS (O2 −, OH−, and HO2 −) by CQD. These free radical groups react with electron-hole pairs, which are expected to form ·OH, corresponding to cell wall degradation, leading to cell machinery failure and death. (ii) Disruption of metabolic processes caused by CQD interaction with target molecules on the bacterial cell wall. (iii) Interaction of CQD with DNA replication in the microbial cell resulting in cell death [62,64,65].

The antioxidant activity of CQD is of paramount importance from a commercial point of view, given its potential application in the food industry. Several research groups have elucidated CQDs' free radical scavenging ability at the cellular level and proposed mechanisms. For example, a fluorescent N, S-CQD concentration-dependent free radical scavenging activity was studied towards Lucigenin-CL assay and DPPH free radicals using electrochemical measurements and UV–vis light absorption [66]. They demonstrated that co-doped CQDs showed antioxidant activity in the xanthine/xanthine oxidase reaction and ROS generation, such as superoxide anion radicals, detected by the Lucigenin-CL assay. In another study, we compared the antioxidant activity of CQD and heteroatom (B, S, and N) doped CQDs with ascorbic acid using (ABTS) and DPPH assays [63]. Undoped CQDs exhibited 100% ABTS and DPPH free radical scavenging activity at 50 μg/mL, whereas the antioxidant activity of ascorbic acid at this concentration was 80% and 53%. N-doped CQDs exhibited 65% and 30% antioxidant activity against ABTS and DPPH at a much lower concentration of 12 μg/mL, while ascorbic acid exhibited ∼20% scavenging capacity in both free radical scavenging methods (Fig. 4 ). The remarkable antioxidant activity of CQDs is due to the presence of surface hydroxyl groups, doping martial, and high dispersibility in an aqueous solution. The free-radical scavenging activity of CQDs is due to ROS formation. Such free radicals can be reduced and quenched by electron transfer, hydrogen donation, and adduct formation from the hydroxyl, nitric oxide, peroxynitrite, and superoxide anion superoxide of the CQDs via extracellular and intracellular reactions [14,44,67,68]. As a result, it suggests that CQD has high potential in various applications such as biological systems and food packaging, where antioxidant activity is required.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo toxicity
ce:section.ce:para: Before using CQDs in food packaging or biomedical applications, it is necessary to investigate their cytotoxicity to ensure safety. The main goal of conducting cytotoxicity tests will be to determine biocompatibility and to gain insight into whether using CQD-based nanocomposites can have potentially detrimental cytotoxic effects. CQDs are most likely biocompatible nanomaterials because they are made from non-toxic raw materials and completely eco-friendly methods. Many research groups have performed the cytotoxicity of food-derived CQDs in everyday food items, including coffee, pizza, beer, bread, cola, honey, salmon, roast duck, hamburgers, barbecue, lamb, etc. [28]. However, in vitro and in vivo toxicity valuation of CQD is imperative in food packaging from a potential application perspective. So far, the cytotoxicity of CQDs has been published several times at the cellular level. Cailotto et al. (2018) performed a CellTiter-Glo® Luminescence assay to test the biocompatibility of glucose, fructose, and ascorbic acid CQD towards HeLa cells for 96 h with the Infinite 200 PRO instrument (Tecan) [69]. The cells retained high viability even at glucose-based CQD concentration of 1000 μg/mL, whereas ascorbic acid-based CQD displayed good biocompatibility at low concentrations (< 250 μg/mL).

On the other hand, the fructose-based CQD was toxic due to carcinogenic furanic compounds. Sun et al. (2021) also performed an MTT [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide] assay to determine the in vitro cytotoxicity of s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs towards mouse fibroblast cell line (L929 cell) and observed the cell viability over 95% even when exposed to concentration as high as 150 μg/mL [70]. Similar low cytotoxicity of citric acid-derived nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CQDs against EA hy926 endothelial cells and nitrogen-doped CQDs against Madin-Darby Canine Kidney and HeLa cells has been reported [66,71]. The in vitro and in vivo toxicity of the N-CQDs were determined by Zhao et al. (2019), who developed CQD using glucose and DETA as nitrogen sources. For in vitro cytotoxicity, the viability of mammalian cells such as HeLa and PANC-1 exposed to different concentrations of N-CQD was determined by MTT assays for 24 h [72]. They observed <80% cell viability at a 0.128 mg/mL concentration with high toxicity at higher concentrations in the two cell lines. The in vivo toxicity tests were carried out on mice by exposing them to 5 mg/mL N-CQD solution for 7 days and observing the morphology of five main organs such as the heart, kidney, lung, spleen, and liver using an optical microscope. They observed no pathological change in any tissue treated with N-CQD, which is explained by N-CQD conversion into non-toxic substances due to digestion. However, the degree of biocompatibility varies depending on the properties of the doping agent and its interaction with the biological membranes. Also, similar types of CQDs may have different interactions with other cells. The concentration-dependent in vitro cytotoxicity of N-doped CQD was studied against mouse fibroblast L929 cells. The results were compared to the cytotoxicity of glucose-based CQD. At the high N-CQD concentration of 500 μg/mL, the cell viability was 80%, and at the much lower concentration, 100 μg/mL, the cell viability was 90%.

Nevertheless, at a 500 μg/mL concentration, Zhao et al. (2019) found relatively low cell viability of HeLa and PANC-1 cells, with ∼30 and 35% cell viability of HeLa and PANC-1 cells. The proposed reason for the different cytotoxicity is the surface modification materials, indicating urea modification was safer than DETA. The interaction of CQDs with biological systems is a rather complex phenomenon. Results depend on the cell system being studied and the assay method used for the cytotoxicity assay. Moreover, this interaction depends on several other factors such as size, surface coating, surface charge, etc. Nevertheless, in most cases, cytotoxicity studies of CQDs performed to date show that concentrations of 10–100 μg/mL cause only a slight loss in cell viability (∼10–20%), which is expected to be safe for biomedical and packaging applications.

Recently, interest in CQD applications in biomedical and lighting devices with excellent performance and lifetime is increasing. For example, bioimaging with COD finds a wide range of applications in biomedical research. The demands for tunable and stable emission, high biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity make developing novel fluorescent materials to achieve high-performance sensors essential. Pure elemental dots are suitable for sensing applications due to their stable and strong luminescence properties. However, several QDs are still limited by problems with poor fluorescence performance [73]. Among the various QDs, CQDs are receiving widespread attention as unique fluorescence sensors. CQDs have demonstrated in vivo analytical and real-time visualization applications to date, indicating their ability for in vivo imaging applications. CQDs have shown an excellent preference for sensing applications due to their unique non-toxicity, hydrophilicity, ultra-small size, stable optical properties, and surface functional groups [74].

On the other hand, CQDs have unique potential properties such as an easy and economical synthesis method, low operating voltage, and high color purity [75]. Metalloid or non-metal QDs have been used for next-generation LEDs. With the advent of effective diodes, quantum dots capable of adjusting the size of the emission wavelength, operation stability, high efficiency, and color purity of high purity have been developed explosively, increasing efficiency. The synthesis of CQDs with useful optical properties has been achieved to meet the commercial needs of applying these novel quantum materials for LED development. Fig. 5 summarizes the current applications of CQDs reported so far.

Currently, few studies have applied CQD-based films to active food packaging. Because the current scenario of food packaging calls for sustainable and safe multifunctional materials, we propose CQD for food preservation from the following perspectives.

Color is one of the basic functions of packaging film that has an important effect on the appearance characteristics and protects the packaged food from photochemical reactions, and plays an important role in determining consumers' first impression. The film's color is greatly affected by adding fillers such as CQD. Since CQDs themselves are brown, incorporation of CQDs into a polymer matrix results in a visually significant color change, but the color properties of CQD-added composite films can vary from polymer to polymer depending on the color of the polymer matrix. Three stimulus color systems can be used to measure the optical property of the packaging films; L-value (lightness), a-value (greenness/redness), and b-value (blueness/yellowness) [94]. Ezati, Roy, et al. (2021) studied the effect of CQD derived from turmeric on the color values of pectin/gelatin-based films [12]. By incorporating CQD and S-CQD, the L-value of the composite film decreased from 87.8 to 85.7 and 80.2, but the a-value increased from −0.8 to −1.1 and 3.3, and the b-value increased from 17.7 to 27.5 and 29.4. Despite adding a small amount of CQD (2%), the whiteness and yellowness indices of the film were significantly reduced, showing that the pectin/gelatin film became brownish-yellow by adding CQD and S-CQD. Roy et al. (2021) showed that gelatin/carrageen-based film became darker (lower L-value) as CQD added increased from 1 to 5%. The L-values of the films decreased from 91.2 to 88.9, the whiteness index increased from 92.5 to 86.8, but the b-values increased from 6.9 to 12.8 [55]. In conclusion, it can be seen that the effect of CQD on the film color varies according to the CQD concentration, quantum yield, and the color of the polymer matrix.

In general, the transparency of food packaging films is measured by their light transmittance in the 200–800 nm range. The light transmittance of the film at 280 and 660 nm is usually used to evaluate the film's transparency and UV blocking properties [95]. Transparent packaging film has the advantage of easy observation and quality evaluation of food contents, but it can transmit light, reducing the quality of food sensitive to photodegradation. Therefore, protecting food by blocking certain wavelengths of light can extend the shelf life of products. For example, light-shielding packaging films prevent photochemical reactions. Several biomaterials and nanoparticles have been used to absorb and scatter light in packaging materials, where CQDs block more short-wavelength UV light than long-wavelength visible light in composite films, suggesting that films containing CQDs increase UV-blocking properties without significantly changing the transparency of the film. In this regard, various polymers have been used as matrices for CQDs to show the UV blocking properties of composite films. These include polymers such as pectin/gelatin [12], gelatin/carrageenan [55], regenerated cellulose [79], PVA [77,84], zein [87], bacterial nanocellulose [60,76]. For instance, Hess et al. (2017) investigated the UV protection capacity of PVA/polyethyleneimine/citric acid-based films containing CQD. The composite film showed >90% protection against UV-A radiation [77]. Recently, Ezati, Roy, et al. (2021) prepared a composite film using pectin and gelatin with varying ratios of CQD and found that composite films containing sulfur functionalized CQD showed excellent UV protection properties [12]. The light transmittance of the composite film decreased from 6.4 to 0.3 at 280 nm and from 89.5 to 61.1 at 660 nm. Similar UV barrier properties have also been studied by Roy et al. (2021) using gelatin/carrageen-based films containing different concentrations of CQD [55]. The UV-light blocking property and photostability of CQD are somehow attributed to the presence of surface functional groups on CQD and the synthesis protocols following the used precursors (dopant elements) that depend on the orbital electrons transition of either π to π* or n to π*. The excited-state intramolecular proton transfer by O-H-O, O-H-N, etc., tunnels, and ample conjugated structures are responsible for the barrier property in the CQD-incorporated films [89,96].

The mechanical properties of the composite film are mostly affected by the type and concentration of the filler, the interaction between the film matrix and the filler, and the internal structure of the film matrix itself. An ideal active film has sufficient mechanical properties to maintain its integrity and prevent packaging failures during production, storage, and distribution [97]. The mechanical properties of a packaging film are usually determined using tensile properties such as TS, EB, and EM [98]. The TS of a composite film is strongly dependent on the filler's compatibility with the polymer matrix and its distribution state in the polymer matrix. When the hydrophobic nanoparticles exceeding the above limit are used in a high concentration, aggregates are formed in the film, the ordered molecular arrangement of the film matrix is destroyed, and the film strength is lowered because the fillers are not evenly distributed in the polymer matrix [99]. Therefore, fillers with multifunctional properties such as antibacterial action, surface hydrophobicity, and biocompatibility are required. Due to their high hydrophilicity, various surface chemistries, and small size, CQDs can be incorporated into biopolymers and act as chemical or physical crosslinkers [79]. However, it is worth mentioning that the important role of the charge on CQDs concerning film compatibility can create aggregation or strong hydrogen bonding during film preparation. It is reported that the mechanical properties of composite films increase or decrease according to the CQD interaction. For instance, Ezati, Roy, et al. (2021) reported an increase in the mechanical properties of pectin/gelatin-based films with the incorporation of CQDs [12].

In contrast, an opposite trend was observed due to the formation of crystal structures when sulfur-functionalized CQDs were added. In another study, Roy et al. (2021) reported that hydrophilic CQDs established strong hydrogen bonds with gelatin/carrageen films and significantly increased TS at concentrations up to 5% [55]. On the other hand, adding anionic CQDs to chitosan with cationic properties resulted in brittle films [100]. Overall, such different effects of CQD depending on the polymer are mainly related to the different compatibility, which reverts directly to the electrostatic interactions and added concentration of CQDs.

Films with high vapor barrier properties can effectively extend the shelf life of packaging products sensitive to moisture changes. Packaged food's chemical and microbiological stability is greatly influenced by the moisture content, which is affected by the moisture movement through the packaging film [101]. A series of processes are involved in the water vapor transmission through a film: (1) adsorption of water vapor on the film surface, (2) diffusion of the water vapor through the film, and (3) evaporation of the vapor at the other side of the film. Additionally, the environment's humidity, temperature, and the film's hydrophilicity can affect the water vapor transmission rate [102,103]. The polymer structure arrangement also affects the water vapor passing through the film, determining the water vapor diffusion path length. One of the disadvantages of incorporating CQD into packaging film is the reduced water vapor barrier properties, as observed in various packaging films such as rapeseed protein, gelatin/carrageen, and gelatin/pectin [12,55,104]. The increased water vapor transmission rate of composite films with CQDs could be attributed to the hydrophilicity of CQDs and the increased water vapor transmission rate due to the formation of hydrogen bonds with the polymer matrix.

The biocompatibility of functional fillers is essential for applying composite films in food packaging applications. In order to apply CQD to food packaging, it is very important to secure the safety of CQD because the food packaging material comes in direct contact with the food, and the filler moves into the food and enters the digestive tract. The toxicity of CQDs in films often depends on several factors such as the size, shape, concentration of nanoparticles, and the type of film substrate. Several studies have shown negligible in vitro cytotoxicity of CQD against EA hy926 endothelial cells, Madin-Darby Canine Kidney, HeLa cells, CHO cell line, HepG2 cells, and L929 mouse fibroblast cells, etc. [43,70,71]. These findings suggest that cell viability was not affected when the CQD concentration was administered at 20 mg/mL, revealing basic information regarding the safety of CQDs. Although, CQD may have toxic effects. Hence, examining the cytotoxicity of packaging films is of paramount importance to understanding the potentially detrimental cytotoxic effects of CQD-based composite films, especially for use in packaging films that tend to enter food and be consumed unintentionally. Recently, we prepared glucose-based CQDs and N-doped CQDs and found that they were biocompatible and non-toxic in CNF blend films on L929 mouse fibroblasts even after prolonged exposure for 72 h [91]. In addition, it was confirmed that there were no pathological changes in the cells treated with CQDs and N-CQDs through digital optical microscopy images, proving the biocompatibility of CQD synthesized from non-toxic raw materials when manufactured in an eco-friendly way.

The recent trend in food packaging research is to develop multifunctional packaging films such as antimicrobial, antioxidant, and UV-blocking properties to secure food safety and maintain quality to extend the shelf life of foods [105,106]. Ezati & Rhim (2020b) used sulfur nanoparticles for antibacterial activity and curcumin for antioxidant activity in pectin-based films [94]. Priyadarshi, Kim, et al. (2021) used ZnONPs for antimicrobial activity and GSE for antioxidant function in CMC-based films [107]. The multifunctional films were used to extend packed meat's shelf life for 15 days, where ZnONPs prevented the growth of microorganisms and GSE prevented fat oxidation. Since few inexpensive multifunctional materials have various functions, two or more functional fillers are mixed to obtain multifunctional packaging materials. In this regard, Das Purkayastha et al. reported the synthesis of CQDs using agricultural waste and their use in the pristine protein-based sachets for food packaging applications [104]. The film showed excellent antioxidant activity at 2–3 mg/mL concentrations. Applying sachets made of CQD and protein composite film to rapeseed oil significantly reduced oxidative rancidity. Natural antioxidant materials such as ascorbic acid, essential oils, polyphenolic compounds, and natural plant extracts have been used to impart antioxidant activity to the food packaging polymers. Recently, Roy et al. (2021) reported the synthesis of CDs using Enoki mushroom and their use in gelatin/carrageenan-based film [55]. The multifunctional composite films exhibited strong antioxidant activity. Also, they confirmed the high antioxidant activity of pectin/gelatin films incorporated with CD from turmeric and GCD and NGCD-embedded CNF film. In these studies, CQDs and polymer matrix enhanced antioxidant activity by inducing effective free radical scavenging ability. However, the CQD-added films showed higher activity against ABTS than the DPPH method due to the higher swelling rate and structural integrity of the polymers in the aqueous solution (ABTS method) than in the methanolic solution (DPPH method) [12]. Therefore, these results demonstrate that CQD-based films are more effective in food packaging as a potentially potent antioxidant.

The antimicrobial activity of a packaging film is the most widely used functional property in active food packaging applications. It is essential in securing food safety and extending shelf life by inhibiting the growth and contamination of food spoilage and pathogenic microorganisms. Except for chitosan, most biopolymers used to make films do not have antimicrobial activity [108]. Therefore, attempts have been made to use functional nanoparticles effective against limited microbial strains to confer antimicrobial activity to packaging films. In contrast, CQDs have broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity against bacterial and fungal strains. As an antimicrobial substance, the efficacy of CQDs has been demonstrated against food-borne microorganisms and a wide range of pathogens such as bacteria, molds, and yeasts [109–112]. The antimicrobial function of the CQD-added film is due to the excellent antimicrobial activity of CQD. It is hypothesized that ROS, such as hydroxyl and superoxide ions, are responsible for the antimicrobial action of CQD. ROS reacts with polyunsaturated phospholipids in the cell membrane leading to microbial death [113–115].

Table 1 also shows various biopolymer-based active food packaging films developed using the antimicrobial properties of CQD. Various methods are used to determine the antimicrobial activity of films, such as well diffusion, disk diffusion, optical density, and viable colony count methods. Kousheh et al. (2020) used CQD to design efficient antibacterial packaging film by absorption/coating method [60]. Using a hydrothermal method, they synthesized CQDs using a cell-free supernatant of Lactobacillus acidophilus and incorporated them at various concentrations (1–500 mg/mL) into bacterial nanocellulose. They tested the antimicrobial activity using the disk diffusion method and reported that the addition of CQD to the polymer matrix led to more effective inhibitory activity on L. monocytogenes than E. coli with a diameter of 18.44 and 12.82 mm, respectively, at 72 mg/cm2 CQD. Later, Salimi et al. (2021) prepared CQDs using white mulberry (Morus alba L.) and used them in nanocellulose sheet blend films by coating time/temperature method for food packaging application [88]. The agar well and disk diffusion methods showed significant antimicrobial activity against L. monocytogenes. These initial findings provided an encouraging opportunity to explore future CQD-based packaging films' development and applications. In a recent study, Wang et al. (2022) showed the potential of CQDs in developing antimicrobial films [90]. They used citric acid, phytic acid, and urea via hydrothermal methods to achieve N, P-CQDs to fabricate chitosan films by solution casting. The chitosan film containing 4% NP-CQDs exhibited 90% UV transmission prevention and effective inhibition against E. coli and S. aureus. Ezati, Roy, et al. (2021) also developed a pectin/gelatin-based film containing turmeric-based CD and S-CD [55]. The films containing S-CD had effective antimicrobial activity against L. monocytogenes and E. coli.

Moreover, the antimicrobial property of GCD and NGCD in cellulose nanofiber was investigated against L. monocytogenes, E. coli, and A. flavus through a colony count method. Interestingly, the film containing NGCD showed the highest antimicrobial activity, especially against fungus [91]. In conclusion, the antimicrobial properties of CQDs are attributed to their small size, surface capping, and high hydrophilicity, along with the presence of surface functional groups in CQDs. CQDs can exert antimicrobial activity through various mechanisms, including ROS generation, causing cytoplasmic leakage through cell-damaging microbial cell membranes, and interfering with metabolic processes by reducing bacterial intracellular ATP concentrations. CQDs with strong antibacterial properties are expected to facilitate the application of these quantum materials in packaging applications.

Active packaging with functional materials provides antibacterial and antioxidant properties to prevent microbial contamination of packaged foods for maintaining food quality and ensuring safety. The active film can be applied directly as a food packaging film or edible coating formulation. The edible coating forms a thin active film protective layer around the food by immersing the food directly in the film-forming solution or spraying the film-forming solution onto the food surface. CQDs are one of the potential antibacterial materials for developing edible coatings and are interested in packaging applications of fresh foods such as fruits and vegetables to extend post-harvest shelf life. There are several application efficiencies of active films containing CQDs in food preservation (Fig. 7 ). Applying a chitosan coating solution composed of kelp-derived CQDs improved the quality of fresh cucumbers at 4 °C, substantially reduced weight loss, and inhibited peroxidase activity [82]. The chitosan coating with 4.5% CQD inhibited the growth of molds and yeasts during storage for 15 days (Fig. 7a).

Similarly, applying CQD-added chitosan-based coating formulations to fresh cucumbers effectively improved shelf life and maintained quality properties [116]. Zhao et al. (2020) used various concentrations of banana-derived CQD (4–8%) and chitosan solutions to extend the shelf life of soymilk (Fig. 7b) [117]. The sample treated with 0.16% chitosan and 8% CQD inhibited the growth of E. coli, S. aureus, and B. subtilis during 4 days of storage, extending the milk shelf life up to 4 days. Patil et al. (2020) also developed a UV-barrier film using spent tea residue-based CQD in the PVA film [84]. When 3 mg/mL of CQD was added, the film prevented light transmittance completely in the range of 230–315 nm. The CQD-added film completely blocked UV-C and UV-B transmission, and when this film was used for packaging grapes, the quality of grapes was improved by UV blocking by the film. We recently tested CQD-added CNF film to coat tangerine and strawberries to extend the shelf life [91]. The coated fruits significantly inhibited the mold growth after coating with NGCD at room temperature. The coating was highly effective against L. monocytogenes, E. coli, and Aspergillus flavus, and the coated citrus fruits were preserved for 15 and 4 days, where NGCD effectively inhibited fungal growth on the fruit surface (Fig. 7c). The antibacterial activity of CQD could inhibit microbial growth, which was very effective in delaying the development of post-harvest fruit rot (Fig. 7d). CQD prolongs the shelf life of fruits by providing antibacterial properties through ROS generation and inhibiting microbial growth. The antibacterial activity of CQD can be effectively used to secure food safety and extend the shelf life of food.

Regarding the application of CQD in the food packaging field, CQD has been demonstrated to act as an active additive due to its excellent antibacterial and antioxidant activity along with biocompatibility. In addition, for CQD-based composites, the efficiency of optical properties is improved, and the cytotoxicity is low. Utilizing surface functional groups, CQDs can be easily incorporated into polymer matrices to form biocompatible packaging films. The promising beneficial properties of CQD-based composite films could be revolutionary because CQDs are responsible for enhanced functional and physicochemical properties, including thermal and mechanical stability, as well as antibacterial and antioxidant functions. These unique advantages allow designing composite films according to the desired application.

The current food packaging market and research environment demand safe, sustainable, and economical multifunctional packaging materials. CQD has recently emerged as a promising prospective material in this regard. Without harmful chemicals, CQDs can be easily synthesized using a simple and easy one-step method. Recently, sustainable materials such as agricultural and food processing wastes and by-products are being used as raw materials for CQD. This approach was driven by the fact that carbon dots are readily generated during the routine cooking of foods. The conditions created during the cooking process are similar to those required for the quantum dot synthesis processes such as pyrolysis, hydrothermal, and microwave-assisted carbonization. Several foods, such as meat, seafood, poultry, and dairy products, have been successfully used to produce CQDs that exhibit properties similar to chemically produced CQDs. In addition, by-products that have been thrown away in the food industry, such as eggshells, feathers, muscle, bone, and blood, are also used to synthesize fluorescent CQDs and are utilized in various applications.

CQDs are probably the most sustainable quantum dots because they are manufactured using carbon sources abundantly available in nature. The purpose of this review was to emphasize that using organic wastes such as food or food processing wastes as raw materials to synthesize CQDs without costly organic matter reduces carbon emissions and enables sustainable development. In addition, most organic and biological wastes contain proteins that aid in the natural surface functionalization of CQDs, depending on the types of amino acids present in the raw material. For example, CQDs prepared using β-keratin-rich feather waste discarded from poultry processing can be functionalized with sulfur-containing surface groups since β-keratin is rich in S-containing amino acids such as methionine and cysteine. In addition, CQDs synthesized from chitinous crab shells can be N-functionalized due to the abundance of amide groups in chitin and chitosan. These possibilities make CQD a safe, sustainable, economical, and versatile value-added quantum dot.

Depending on the raw material from which CQDs are manufactured, there is tremendous potential for engineering due to the wide range of functionalizations of CQDs. In addition, the size of the CQDs can be adjusted by varying the synthesis method and conditions. For example, it has been reported that increasing the temperature during pyrolysis reduces CQD size and improves PLQY. Also, the properties of CQDs can be tuned through functionalization and surface modification with functional materials. In summary, this article showed that CQDs could be synthesized from food ingredients using environmentally friendly methods that can increase the economics and sustainability of the food supply chain. The interaction between CQDs and the surface functional groups created by the polymer matrix contributes to the successful incorporation of CQDs into packaging materials. Functionalization also helps interactions with biological surfaces, creating tremendous potential for biomedical and food packaging applications. CQDs have been used to preserve packaged foods through their functional activity and modulate packaging films' physicochemical properties. CQDs have low in vitro and in vivo toxicity and high antibacterial, antifungal, and antioxidant activity, making them a potential material for use as active materials in direct contact food packaging and biological system applications. With the development of advanced technology and characterization, they have opened up a new research area that will greatly extend the application scope of CQD-based materials. Simply put, this prospect shows that CQDs have unique properties and incredible potential in food packaging applications. However, much research needs to be done to establish the economics of CQDs in large-scale production for various applications.
